perturbation.original_text: Converge shall pay the first such Monthly Installment to VNE on the date this Agreement is executed and delivered by both parties. Thereafter, Converge shall pay each such Monthly Installment to VNE no later than the first day of each month during the Initial Term (November 2001 and through March 2003). All such Monthly Installments are non-refundable.
perturbation.changed_text: Converge shall pay the first such Monthly Installment to VNE 90 days after the date this Agreement is executed and delivered by both parties. Thereafter, Converge shall pay each such Monthly Installment to VNE no later than the 30th day of each quarter during the Initial Term (November 2001 and through March 2003). All such Monthly Installments are non-refundable.
perturbation.explanation: This modification alters the payment schedule, potentially conflicting with prompt payment laws if VNE is considered a small business or subcontractor.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 31 U.S. Code § 3903 - Interest penalties for late payments
perturbation.law_explanation: The Prompt Payment Act requires federal agencies to pay contractors on time. Changing the payment terms to 90 days after execution may violate these requirements if VNE is a small business or subcontractor providing services to a federal agency through Converge. The new terms could trigger interest penalties under the Act.

Regulations (a) The Director of the Office of Management and Budget shall prescribe regulations to carry out section 3902 of this title . The regulations shall- (1) provide that the required payment date is- (A) the date payment is due under the contract for the item of property or service provided; or (B) except as provided in paragraphs (10) and (11), 30 days after a proper invoice for the amount due is received if a specific payment dat
perturbation.justification: The modification delays the initial payment to 90 days after the agreement's execution and changes subsequent payments to the 30th day of each quarter. This contradicts the Prompt Payment Act if VNE is a small business or subcontractor. 31 USC 3903(a)(1)(B) states that regulations should provide that the required payment date is 30 days after a proper invoice is received. The changed text extends this period significantly, potentially violating the Act and triggering interest penalties as described in the Law Explanation. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists because the modified payment terms clash with the federal regulation concerning prompt payment.

perturbation.original_text: In the event of any breach of the foregoing warranty, and provided that Converge reports such breach to VNE in writing within 90 days following the date of performance of the Services in question, VNE shall, as its sole obligation and Converge's sole and exclusive remedy, promptly repair, replace or re-perform the Services in question, without additional cost to Converge, so as to correct the warranty non-compliance as promptly as practicable (within 30 days to the extent technically feasible).
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of any breach of the foregoing warranty, and provided that Converge reports such breach to VNE in writing within 30 days following the date of performance of the Services in question, VNE shall, as its sole obligation and Converge's sole and exclusive remedy, promptly repair, replace or re-perform the Services in question, without additional cost to Converge, so as to correct the warranty non-compliance as promptly as practicable (within 90 days to the extent technically feasible).
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the reporting window to 30 days and extending the correction period to 90 days may be considered unconscionable and violate consumer protection laws, particularly if the services are complex.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 2304 - Performance of obligations under warranties
perturbation.law_explanation: The Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act requires that warranty terms be reasonable and not deceptive. A 30-day reporting window for breaches, coupled with a potentially lengthy 90-day correction period, may be deemed unreasonable, especially for complex services. This could violate the Act's consumer protection provisions.

Federal minimum standards for warranties (a) Remedies under written warranty; duration of implied warranty; exclusion or limitation on consequential damages for breach of written or implied warranty; election of refund or replacement In order for a warrantor warranting a consumer product by means of a written warranty to meet the Federal minimum standards for warranty- (1) such warrantor must as a minimum remedy such consumer product within a reasonable time and without char
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters the original contract by shortening the reporting window for breaches from 90 to 30 days and extending the correction period from 30 to 90 days. The law explanation suggests this may be unreasonable under the Magnuson-Moss Warranty Act, especially for complex services. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 2304, states that a warrantor must remedy a consumer product within a reasonable time. A 30-day reporting window coupled with a 90-day correction period may not meet the "reasonable time" standard, thus creating a contradiction. The original text gave 90 days for the consumer to report, and 30 days for the company to fix it, which is more reasonable then shortening the reporting window to 30 and extending the repair time to 90.

perturbation.original_text: Unless otherwise provided by local law without the possibility of contractual waiver or limitation, any legal or other action related to this Agreement must be commenced no later than two years from the date on which the cause of action arose.
perturbation.changed_text: Unless otherwise provided by local law without the possibility of contractual waiver or limitation, any legal or other action related to this Agreement must be commenced no later than six months from the date on which the cause of action arose.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the statute of limitations to six months is against contract law principles.
perturbation.law_explanation: According to 12 Pa.C.S. § 2725, an action for breach of any contract for sale must be commenced within four years after the cause of action has accrued. Reducing the statute of limitations to six months would violate this law.
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies a two-year limitation for legal actions, while the changed text reduces this to six months. The Law Explanation references 12 Pa.C.S. § 2725, stating that a four-year statute of limitations applies to contract breaches. Since the changed text sets a six-month limit, it contradicts the four-year requirement, making the contractual term unenforceable as it violates state law. This aligns with the definition of 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction,' where a time-sensitive obligation in a contract violates federal or state law.
